subject: Physics
q: The binding energy per nucleon of deuteron $\left({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}\right)$ and helium nucleus $\left({ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}\right)$ is $1.1 \mathrm{MeV}$ and $7 \mathrm{MeV}$ respectively. If two deuteron nuclei react to form a single helium nucleus, then the energy released is
question: The binding energy per nucleon of deuteron $\left({ }_1^2 \mathrm{H}\right)$ and helium nucleus $\left({ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}\right)$ is $1.1 \mathrm{MeV}$ and $7 \mathrm{MeV}$ respectively. If two deuteron nuclei react to form a single helium nucleus, then the energy released is
solution: Energy released $=$ total binding energy of product $-$ total binding energy of reactants $\Rightarrow 28-(2 \times 2.2)=28-4.4=236 \mathrm{MeV}$

subject: Physics
q: A sample contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each of two substances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with half lives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of their atomic weights is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of the amounts of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
question: A sample contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> each of two substances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> with half lives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> respectively. The ratio of their atomic weights is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>2</mn></math>. The ratio of the amounts of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>100</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Ratio of number of moles of substance in the beginning will be, N0AN0B=10-2 kgMA10-2 kgMB=MBMA=12.

Number of half-life in 16 s for element A will be, nA=164=4

Number of half-life in 16 s for element B will be, nB=168=2

Hence, ratio of number of nuclei after 16 s will be, =N0A×12nAN0B×12nB=12×124-2=18=25100

Thus, value of x is 25.

q: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts which have their velocities in the ratio $2: 1$. The ratio of their nuclear sizes will be
question: A nucleus disintegrates into two nuclear parts which have their velocities in the ratio $2: 1$. The ratio of their nuclear sizes will be
solution:
$\frac{R_1}{R_2}=\left(\frac{m_2}{2 m_2}\right)^{1 / 3}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{R_1}{R_2}=1: 2^{1 / 3}$

subject: Physics
q:
Choose the correct nuclear process from the below options
[p: proton, n : neutron, $\mathrm{e}^{-}$: electron, $\mathrm{e}^{+}$: positron, $v:$ neutrino, $\bar{v}$ : antineutrino]
question:
Choose the correct nuclear process from the below options
[p: proton, n : neutron, $\mathrm{e}^{-}$: electron, $\mathrm{e}^{+}$: positron, $v:$ neutrino, $\bar{v}$ : antineutrino]
solution:
For all nuclear processes, charge must be conserved. Also, when a release of an electron ( $e^{-}$) is always accompanied by a release of an antineutrino ( $\bar{v}$ )

Hence, $n \rightarrow p+\mathrm{e}^{-}+\overline{\mathrm{v}}$ is the correct answer.

q: How many alpha and beta particles are emitted when Uranium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mprescripts/><mn>92</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>238</mn></msup></math> decays to lead <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>Pb</mi></mrow><mprescripts/><mn>82</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>206</mn></msup></math>?
question: How many alpha and beta particles are emitted when Uranium <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mprescripts/><mn>92</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>238</mn></msup></math> decays to lead <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mmultiscripts><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>Pb</mi></mrow><mprescripts/><mn>82</mn><none/></mmultiscripts><mn>206</mn></msup></math>?
solution:
U92238→Pb82206

Change in mass number is 4α=238-206=32⇒α=8

Change in atomic number is -2α+β=-10⇒-16+β=-10⇒β=6

subject: Physics
q: The half-life of a radioactive element $\mathrm{A}$ is the same as the mean-life of another radioactive element B. Initially both substances have the same number of atoms, then :
question: The half-life of a radioactive element $\mathrm{A}$ is the same as the mean-life of another radioactive element B. Initially both substances have the same number of atoms, then :
options: A and B decay at the same rate always.
A and $\mathrm{B}$ decay at the same rate initially.
A will decay at a faster rate than B.
$\mathrm{B}$ will decay at a faster rate than $\mathrm{A}$.
solution:
$\left(\mathrm{T}_{1 / 2}\right)_{\mathrm{A}}=\left(\mathrm{t}_{\text {mean }}\right)_{\mathrm{B}}$
$$
\Rightarrow \frac{0.693}{\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{1}{\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}} \Rightarrow \lambda_{\mathrm{A}}=0.693 \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}
$$
or $\lambda_{\mathrm{A}} < \lambda_{\mathrm{B}}$
Also rate of decay $=\lambda \mathrm{N}$
Initially number of atoms $(\mathrm{N})$ of both are equal but since $\lambda_{\mathrm{B}}>\lambda_{\mathrm{A}}$, therefore $\mathrm{B}$ will decay at a faster rate than $\mathrm{A}$

subject: Physics
q:
Imagine that a reactor converts all the given mass into energy and that it operates at a power level of 109 Watt . The mass of the fuel consumed per hour, in the reactor, will be:
(velocity of light, c is 3×108 m s-1)
question:
Imagine that a reactor converts all the given mass into energy and that it operates at a power level of 109 Watt . The mass of the fuel consumed per hour, in the reactor, will be:
(velocity of light, c is 3×108 m s-1)
solution:
Power of reactor, P=EΔt=Δmc2Δt(From mass-energy equivalence equation) 

Given in the problem power level =109 Watt .

∆m∆t=Pc2 =1093×1082

⇒ ∆m∆t=10-79 kg s-1 

The mass of fuel consumed per hour=10-79×1000 g3600 h-1=4×10-2 g h-1.

subject: Physics
q:
A nucleus of mass $M+\Delta m$ is at rest and decays into two daughter nuclei of equal mass $\frac{M}{2}$ each. Speed of light is $\mathrm{C}$.
The speed of daughter nuclei is
question:
A nucleus of mass $M+\Delta m$ is at rest and decays into two daughter nuclei of equal mass $\frac{M}{2}$ each. Speed of light is $\mathrm{C}$.
The speed of daughter nuclei is
solution:
Conserving the momentum
$$
\begin{gathered}
0=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_1-\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_2 \\
\mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{V}_2 \quad \quad \ldots \ldots (1) \\
\Delta \mathrm{mc}^2=\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_1^2+\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \cdot \mathrm{V}_2^2 \quad \quad \ldots \ldots (2)\\
\Delta \mathrm{mc}^2=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{2} \mathrm{~V}_1^2 \\
\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{mc}^2}{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{V}_1^2 \\
\mathrm{~V}_1=\mathrm{c} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}}
\end{gathered}
$$

question: If radius of ${ }_{13}^{27} \mathrm{Al}$ nucleus is estimated to be $3.6$ Fermi then the radius ${ }_{52}^{125} \mathrm{Te}$ nucleus be nearly
solution: $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{3.6}=\left(\frac{125}{27}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=6$ fermi

subject: Physics
q: Calculate the time interval between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>33</mn><mo>%</mo></math> decay and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>67</mn><mo>%</mo></math> decay if half-life of a substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>.
question: Calculate the time interval between <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>33</mn><mo>%</mo></math> decay and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>67</mn><mo>%</mo></math> decay if half-life of a substance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>min</mi></math>.
solution:
N1=N0e-λt1

N1 N0=e-λt1

0.67=e-λt1

ln0.67=-λt1

 N2=N0e-λt2

N2 N0=e-λt2

0.33=e-λt2

ln0.33=-λt2

ln0.67-ln0.33=λt1-λt2

λt1-t2=ln0.670.33

λt1-t2≅ln2

t1-t2≃ln2λ=t1/2

Half life =t1/2=20 minutes.

subject: Physics
q: The counting rate observed from a radioactive source at $t=0$ was 1600 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, it was 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The counting rate observed as counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
question: The counting rate observed from a radioactive source at $t=0$ was 1600 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, and $t=8 \mathrm{~s}$, it was 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$. The counting rate observed as counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ at $t=6 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
solution:
As we know,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& {\left[\frac{N}{N_0}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n} \\
& n=\text { no. of half life } \\
& N \text { - no. of atoms left } \\
& N_0 \text { - initial no. of atoms } \\
& \text { By radioactive decay law, } \\
& \frac{d N}{d \mathrm{t}}=k N \\
& k-\operatorname{disin} t e g r a t i o n \text { constant } \\
& \therefore \frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\frac{N}{N_0}
\end{aligned}
$$
From (i) and (ii) we get
$$
\frac{\frac{d N}{d t}}{\frac{d N_0}{d t}}=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n
$$
or, $\left[\frac{100}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n \Rightarrow\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^4=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^n$
$\therefore n=4$, Therefore, in 8 seconds 4 half life had occurred in which counting rate reduces to 100 counts $\mathrm{s}^{-1}$.
$\therefore$ Half life, $\frac{T_1}{2}=2 \mathrm{sec}$
In $6 \mathrm{sec}, 3$ half life will occur
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore\left[\frac{\frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}}{1600}\right]=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{d \mathrm{~N}}{d t}=200 \text { counts } \mathrm{s}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: If a radioactive element having half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> min is undergoing beta decay, the fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn></math> min will be :
question: If a radioactive element having half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> min is undergoing beta decay, the fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>90</mn></math> min will be :
solution:
Given: Half life of radioactive element t12=30 min.

In 90 min, there will be three half lives, so n=3.

Using radioactive decay law, fraction of radioactive element remains undecayed is 

NN0=12n=123

⇒NN0=18

subject: Physics
q: A radioactive element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>92</mn><mn>242</mn></mmultiscripts></math> emits two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particles, one electron and two positrons. The product nucleus is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mn>234</mn></mmultiscripts></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ______.
question: A radioactive element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>92</mn><mn>242</mn></mmultiscripts></math> emits two <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>-particles, one electron and two positrons. The product nucleus is represented by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mprescripts></mprescripts><mi mathvariant="normal">P</mi><mn>234</mn></mmultiscripts></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is ______.
solution:
When a radioactive nucleus emits 1 α-particle, its mass number decreases by 4 and the atomic number decreases by 2. During positron emission, the mass number remains unchanged but the atomic number decreases by 1. Similarly, during electron emission, the mass number remains unchanged but the atomic number decreases by 1.

So, the value of P=92-2-2+1-1-1

⇒P=92-5

⇒P=87.

subject: Physics
q: Two radioactive substances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> originally have <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> nuclei respectively. Half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> is half of the half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>. After three half lives of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>, number of nuclei of both are equal. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be equal to :
question: Two radioactive substances <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math> originally have <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> nuclei respectively. Half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>X</mi></math> is half of the half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>. After three half lives of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Y</mi></math>, number of nuclei of both are equal. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>N</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be equal to :
solution:
Tx=t;Ty=2t

3Ty=6t,

N1'=N2'

N1e-λ16t=N2e-λ26t

N1 N2=eλ1-λ26t=e1t-12t×6tln2=eln23=eln8=8

subject: Physics
q:
The disintegration rate of a certain radioactive sample at any instant is 4250 disintegrations per minute. 10 minutes later, the rate becomes 2250 disintegrations per minute. The approximate decay constant is

(Take loge1.88=0.63)
question:
The disintegration rate of a certain radioactive sample at any instant is 4250 disintegrations per minute. 10 minutes later, the rate becomes 2250 disintegrations per minute. The approximate decay constant is

(Take loge1.88=0.63)
solution:
At t=0 disintegration rate A0=4250 dpm

At t=10 min disintegration rate At=2250dpm

The activity of a sample at any time is given by,

At=A0e-λt

⇒2250=4250e-λ10

⇒10λ=ln42502250=ln1.88

⇒λ=0.063 min-1

subject: Physics
q: A piece of bone of an animal from a ruin is found to have ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ activity of 12 disintegrations per minute per gm of its carbon content. The ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ activity of a living animal is 16 disintegrations per minute per gm. How long ago nearly did the animal die? (Given half life of ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5760$ years)
question: A piece of bone of an animal from a ruin is found to have ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ activity of 12 disintegrations per minute per gm of its carbon content. The ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ activity of a living animal is 16 disintegrations per minute per gm. How long ago nearly did the animal die? (Given half life of ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5760$ years)
solution:
Given, for ${ }^{14} \mathrm{C}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
&A_0=16 \text { dis } \min ^{-1} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} \\
&\mathrm{~A}=12 \text { dis } \min ^{-1} \mathrm{~g}^{-1} \\
&\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}=5760 \text { years }
\end{aligned}
$$
Now, $\lambda=\frac{0.693}{\mathrm{t}_{1 / 2}}$
$\lambda=\frac{0.693}{5760}$ per year
Then, from, $\mathrm{t}=\frac{2.303}{\lambda} \log _{10} \frac{\mathrm{A}_0}{\mathrm{~A}}$
$$
=\frac{2.303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log _{10} \frac{16}{12}
$$
$$
=\frac{2.303 \times 5760}{0.693} \log _{10} 1.333
$$
$=\frac{2.303 \times 5760 \times 0.1249}{0.693}$
$=2390.81 \approx 2391$ years

subject: Physics
q:
A star has $100 \%$ helium composition. It starts to convert three ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into one ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ via triple alpha process as ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$. The mass of the star is $2.0 \times 10^{32} \mathrm{~kg}$ and it generates energy at the rate of $5.808 \times 10^{30} \mathrm{~W}$. The rate of converting these ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ to ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{n} \times 10^{42} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, where $\mathrm{n}$ is _________
[ Take, mass of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}=4.0026 \mathrm{u}$, mass of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=12 \mathrm{u}$ ]
question:
A star has $100 \%$ helium composition. It starts to convert three ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into one ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ via triple alpha process as ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$. The mass of the star is $2.0 \times 10^{32} \mathrm{~kg}$ and it generates energy at the rate of $5.808 \times 10^{30} \mathrm{~W}$. The rate of converting these ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ to ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}$ is $\mathrm{n} \times 10^{42} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$, where $\mathrm{n}$ is _________
[ Take, mass of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}=4.0026 \mathrm{u}$, mass of ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=12 \mathrm{u}$ ]
solution:
${ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He}+{ }^4 \mathrm{He} \rightarrow{ }^{12} \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Q}$
power generated $=\frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}} \mathrm{Q}$
where, $\mathrm{N} \rightarrow$ No. of reaction $/ \mathrm{sec}$.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Q}=\left(3 \mathrm{~m}_{\mathrm{He}}-\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{C}}\right) \mathrm{C}^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=(3 \times 4.0026-12)\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2 \\ & \mathrm{Q}=7.266 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{\text { power }}{\mathrm{Q}}=\frac{5.808 \times 10^{30}}{7.266 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{N}}{\mathrm{t}}=5 \times 10^{42}\end{aligned}$
rate of conversion of ${ }^4 \mathrm{He}$ into ${ }^{12} \mathrm{C}=15 \times 10^{42}$ Hence, $\mathrm{n}=15$

q: The half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Au</mi><mn>198</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn></math> days. The activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Au</mi><mn>198</mn></msup></math>, if its atomic weight is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>198</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The half-life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Au</mi><mn>198</mn></msup></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>7</mn></math> days. The activity of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mg</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Au</mi><mn>198</mn></msup></math>, if its atomic weight is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>198</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> is, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The activity will be

A=λN

Now,

λ=ln2t12 and N=1.5×10-3198×6×1023

Therefore, activity in Ci,

A=ln2t12×1.5×10-3198×6×10233.7×10-10=ln22.7×24×60×60×1.5×10-3198×6×10233.7×10-10=366 Ci≈357 Ci

subject: Physics
q:
The energy released in the fusion of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of hydrogen deep in the sun is $E_H$ and the energy released in the fission of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $E_U$. The ratio $\frac{E_H}{E_U}$ is approximately:
(Consider the fusion reaction as $4 \mid H+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+2 v+6 \gamma+26.7 \mathrm{MeV}$, energy released in the fission reaction of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ per fission nucleus and $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ $\left.6.023 \times 10^{23}\right)$
question:
The energy released in the fusion of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of hydrogen deep in the sun is $E_H$ and the energy released in the fission of $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $E_U$. The ratio $\frac{E_H}{E_U}$ is approximately:
(Consider the fusion reaction as $4 \mid H+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow{ }_2^4 \mathrm{He}+2 v+6 \gamma+26.7 \mathrm{MeV}$, energy released in the fission reaction of ${ }^{235} \mathrm{U}$ is $200 \mathrm{MeV}$ per fission nucleus and $\mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=$ $\left.6.023 \times 10^{23}\right)$
solution:
In each fusion reaction, $4{ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}$ nucleus are used. 
Energy released per Nuclei of ${ }_1^1 \mathrm{H}=\frac{26.7}{4} \mathrm{MeV}$
$\therefore$ Energy released by $2 \mathrm{~kg}$ hydrogen $\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{H}}\right)$
$=\frac{2000}{1} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times \frac{26.7}{4} \mathrm{MeV}$
$\qquad \qquad\quad \&$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Energy released by } 2 \mathrm{~kg} \text { Vranium }\left(\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{V}}\right) \\ & =\frac{2000}{235} \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}} \times 200 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$
So,
$\frac{E_H}{E_V}=235 \times \frac{26.7}{4 \times 200}=7.84$
$\therefore$ Approximately close to 7.62

q: The atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>12</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>000000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>003354</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The required energy to remove a neutron from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math>, if mass of neutron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>008665</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, will be:
question: The atomic mass of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>12</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>000000</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math> and that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>003354</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>. The required energy to remove a neutron from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mmultiscripts><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><none></none><mn>13</mn><mprescripts></mprescripts><mn>6</mn><none></none></mmultiscripts></math>, if mass of neutron is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>008665</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">u</mi></math>, will be:
solution:
Given the nuclear reaction is

C136+Energy→C126+n10

The mass defect for the above reaction can be calculated as follows:

Δm=(12.000000+1.008665)-13.003354 u=-0.00531 u

Hence, the energy required is given by

E=0.00531×931.5 MeV=4.95 MeV

subject: Physics
q: What is the half-life period of a radioactive material if its activity drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>16</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> of its initial value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years ?
question: What is the half-life period of a radioactive material if its activity drops to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mn>16</mn><mrow><mi>th</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></mfrac></math> of its initial value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>30</mn></math> years ?
solution:
Activity of a radioactive material is given by A=A0e-λt, here, λ- is decay constant.

Taking logarithmic function both sides, 

⇒-λt=lnAA0

Using relation between half life and decay constant,

⇒-ln2t12×30=ln116 

 ⇒-ln2t12×30=-4ln2

⇒t12=304=7.5 yrs

question: A radioactive material decays by simultaneous emissions of two particles with half lives of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1400</mn></math> years and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>700</mn></math> years, respectively. What will be the time after the which one third of the material remains ? (Take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mn>3</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>1</mn></math>)
solution:
Given λ1=ℓn2700/year λ2=ℓn21400/year
∴λnet =λ1+λ2=ln21700+11400
=3ln21400/year
Now, let initial number of radioactive nuclei be
N0, 
∴N03=N0e-λmatt

⇒ℓn13=-λnett
⇒1.1=3×0.6931400t⇒t≈740 years
Hence, option 4.

question: The mass number of nucleus having radius equal to half of the radius of nucleus with mass number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>192</mn></math> is:
solution:

q: Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>β</mi></mrow></msub></math> be the number of <math><mi>β</mi></math> particle emitted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mn>24</mn></msup></math> radioactive nuclei having a half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math>. In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math>, the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>β</mi></msub></math> is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.023</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mole</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Let <math><msub><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>β</mi></mrow></msub></math> be the number of <math><mi>β</mi></math> particle emitted by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>gram</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>Na</mi><mn>24</mn></msup></math> radioactive nuclei having a half life of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>15</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math>. In <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>7</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math>, the number <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>N</mi><mi>β</mi></msub></math> is close to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><msub><mi>N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.023</mo><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mole</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
∵NN0=12n
N = no. of un-decayed nuclei
N0 = initial no. of nuclei
Also t=n×T12
⇒NN0=12tT12
⇒N=N0127.515
⇒N=N01212⇒N=N02 
∴ Number of nuclei decayed
=N0-N02=N02-12
=0.4141.414N0
=0.29N0
∵N0=124×6.023×1023
⇒ No. of nuclei decayed =0.29×124×6.023×1023
=0.0727×1023
=7.5×1021

subject: Physics
q: Two radioactive materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> have decay constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to that of A will be "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math>" after a time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is _____ .
question: Two radioactive materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> have decay constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to that of A will be "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math>" after a time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
According to radioactive decay formula, N=N0e-λt, here, N0 is the initial number of nuclei in a radioactive material.

Given here,  λ1=25λ and λ2=16λ.

And NBNA=e

For radioactive material A, NA=N0e-λ1t

For radioactive material B, NB=N0e-λ2t

Thus, the ratio NBNA=e-λ2te-λ1t=e-λ2t·eλ1t

⇒e1=eλ1-λ2t

⇒λ1-λ2t=1

Time, t=1λ1-λ2=125λ-16λ=19λ.

Hence, the value of a=9.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The density of the copper $\left({ }_{29}^{64} \mathrm{Cu}\right)$ nucleus is greater than that of the carbon $\left({ }_6^{12} \mathrm{C}\right)$ nucleus.
Reason (R): The nucleus of mass number A has a radius proportional to $\mathrm{A}^{1 / 3}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : The density of the copper $\left({ }_{29}^{64} \mathrm{Cu}\right)$ nucleus is greater than that of the carbon $\left({ }_6^{12} \mathrm{C}\right)$ nucleus.
Reason (R): The nucleus of mass number A has a radius proportional to $\mathrm{A}^{1 / 3}$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: $(\mathrm{A})$ is correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct
$(\mathrm{A})$ is not correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is correct
Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct and $(\mathrm{R})$ is the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
$\rho=\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{~V}}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{n}} \times \mathrm{A}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{R}^3}=\frac{\mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{n}} \times \mathrm{A}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{AR}_0^3}$
So $\rho$ is almost is constant
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_0 \mathrm{~A}^{1 / 3} \\ & \mathrm{R} \propto \mathrm{~A}^{1 / 3}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
For a nucleus XZA having mass number A and atomic number Z

A. The surface energy per nucleon (bs)=–a1A23.

B. The Coulomb contribution to the binding energy bc=−a2Z(Z−1)A43.

C. The volume energy bv = a3A

D. Decrease in the binding energy is proportional to surface area.

E. While estimating the surface energy, it is assumed that each nucleon interacts with 12 nucleons. (a1, a2 and a3 are constants)

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
For a nucleus XZA having mass number A and atomic number Z

A. The surface energy per nucleon (bs)=–a1A23.

B. The Coulomb contribution to the binding energy bc=−a2Z(Z−1)A43.

C. The volume energy bv = a3A

D. Decrease in the binding energy is proportional to surface area.

E. While estimating the surface energy, it is assumed that each nucleon interacts with 12 nucleons. (a1, a2 and a3 are constants)

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
The formula to calculate the radius of a nucleus is given by

R=R0A13   ...1

Hence, the volume energy can be written as

bv=C1R3= a3A133=a3A

The nuclear binding energy is the minimum energy that is required to disassemble the nucleus of an atom into its constituent protons and neutrons. Also, the energy of the nucleus is negative with regard to the energy of the particles pulled apart to infinite distance, because energy must be utilised to split a nucleus into its individual protons and neutrons. It is proportional to the surface area.

Hence, statements C and D are most appropriate.

subject: Physics
q:
The explosive in a Hydrogen bomb is a mixture of H21,H31 and Li63 in some condensed form. The chain reaction is given by Li63+n10→He42+H31;  H21+H31→He42+n10

During the explosion the energy released is approximately [Given : M(Li)=6.01690 amu, MH21=2.01471 amu, MHe42=4.00388 amu and 1 amu=931.5 MeV]
question:
The explosive in a Hydrogen bomb is a mixture of H21,H31 and Li63 in some condensed form. The chain reaction is given by Li63+n10→He42+H31;  H21+H31→He42+n10

During the explosion the energy released is approximately [Given : M(Li)=6.01690 amu, MH21=2.01471 amu, MHe42=4.00388 amu and 1 amu=931.5 MeV]
solution:
Combining the reactions, it can be written that

Li63+n10→He42+H31

H21+H31→He42+n10

_____________________Li63+H21→2He42

Hence, the energy released in process can be calculated as follows

Q=Δmc2=M(Li)+MH21-2×MHe42×931.5 MeV=[6.01690+2.01471-2×4.00388]×931.5 MeV=22.22 MeV

subject: Physics
q: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
question: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
solution:
The fusion reaction is given by:

$2 ^{2}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

Binding energy of one $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ nucleus = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ MeV

Total initial binding energy = $2 \times 2.2 = 4.4$ MeV

Binding energy of $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ nucleus = $4 \times 7.2 = 28.8$ MeV

Energy released $\Delta E$ = Final binding energy - Initial binding energy

$\Delta E = 28.8 - 4.4 = 24.4$ MeV

Answer: $24.4$
